name: Tucson Daily Brief Local Wordbank
description: Curated 3-5 letter answers with strong Tucson, Sonoran Desert, or Southwest-flavored clue angles. Designed to be layered on top of the Spread The Wordlist (STWL) general-fill list used by the Crosswording the Situation grid generator. The grid filler should *prefer* these words when they fit, but fall back to STWL when they don't. Many of these words also exist in STWL — the value-add here is the metadata (category, clue angles, context) that biases an LLM toward warm, locally-flavored cluing.
notes_for_editor: Answer length is constrained to 3-5 letters because the existing crossword grid is a fixed 5x5 layout with rotationally symmetric black squares at (0,4) and (4,0). Iconic Tucson words longer than 5 letters (SAGUARO, OCOTILLO, MONSOON, MARANA, etc.) live in the thematic_lexicon section below — they belong in clue *text*, not grid answers.

tucson_strength: 'high' = instantly Tucson, only this place reads it that way. 'medium' = Southwest/Arizona/Sonoran flavor, reads local without being unique. 'low' = generic crossword fill that has a natural, warm Tucson clue angle available when wanted.

clue_styles are starting points, not the only options. The LLM may compose its own as long as it stays within tone (warm, fair, brief, no false certainty). For uncertain news items (early filings, planned openings), prefer hedged language: 'planned,' 'proposed,' 'listed,' 'on the agenda.'
context explains *why* the word is local. This is the most valuable field for the LLM and for editorial review.
Avoid using more than 1-2 Spanish loanwords per puzzle, and never cluster them. A 5x5 with NOPAL/ELOTE/ASADA all in one grid will feel forced.
Audit before first production run: confirm spellings, remove anything that feels too obscure for a general Tucson reader, add anything missing. This is v0.1 and meant to grow.

tucson_strength_scale.high: Instantly Tucson, no other place reads this way.
tucson_strength_scale.medium: Southwest / Arizona / Sonoran. Reads local without being unique.
tucson_strength_scale.low: Works in any crossword, but has a natural warm Tucson clue angle.

words.context: 4th Avenue is Tucson's iconic walkable retail/bar strip. Many major Tucson roads are 'Ave' (Campbell, Stone, Park).

clue_styles: Flying mammal that swarms Tucson bridges at dusk
Campbell Avenue bridge resident, in summer
words.context: Tucson is famous for its Mexican free-tailed bat colonies that emerge from bridges (especially Campbell Ave) at dusk in summer. A beloved local sight.

clue_styles: Honey maker buzzing through the saguaro blooms
words.context: Native bees pollinate saguaro and other desert flora; the Sonoran Desert has unusually high native bee diversity.

clue_styles: Tucson's annual February ___ Show draws thousands
words.context: The Tucson Gem & Mineral Show (every February) is the largest of its kind in the world. Locals just call it 'the Gem Show.'

clue_styles: What it is in Tucson from May through September
Like a sidewalk at noon in July
words.context: Tucson summers regularly hit 105-110°F. Universally local context.

clue_styles: Tree species found in the Sky Islands
words.context: The Sky Island mountain ranges around Tucson (Catalinas, Rincons, Santa Ritas) host Mexican blue oak and Emory oak in transition zones above the desert.
La Fiesta de los Vaqueros (Tucson Rodeo) and the strong mariachi tradition make this Spanish exclamation feel local.

clue_styles: What Arizona's old copper mines pulled out of the ground
words.context: Arizona is the 'Copper State' — copper mining heritage and the Tucson Gem & Mineral Show give this strong local color.

clue_styles: Spanish word in '___ Valley' (town north of Tucson)
words.context: Oro Valley is one of the four municipalities TDB covers. 'Oro' = Spanish for gold.

clue_styles: Elf ___ (tiny cactus-dwelling bird of the Sonoran Desert)
words.context: The elf owl (smallest owl in the world) nests in saguaro cavities. Burrowing owls are also iconic Sonoran Desert wildlife.

clue_styles: What a palo verde drops in spring
words.context: Mesquite and palo verde trees produce distinctive seed pods all over Tucson sidewalks and yards.

clue_styles: Word in '___ Nuevo' (downtown Tucson redevelopment district)
Spanish for the Santa Cruz, e.g.
words.context: Rio Nuevo is the downtown Tucson tax district that's funded a lot of recent development. Tucson sits on the Santa Cruz River.

clue_styles: What 350 days of Tucson sky has
Spanish for the thing that runs Tucson's rooftop solar
words.context: Tucson averages ~286 sunny days per year. 'Sol' = Spanish for sun, fits the bilingual local register.

clue_styles: What Tucson gets nearly 300 days a year
words.context: Tucson sun is a defining feature; the Rincon Mountains form the eastern horizon.

clue_styles: Mud-and-straw brick used to build Old Tucson
Building material of Barrio Viejo's historic homes
words.context: Adobe is the traditional building material of Tucson's Sonoran row houses; Barrio Viejo is full of original adobe homes. Avoid the software-company clue angle in this puzzle.

clue_styles: Spiky desert succulent that gives us tequila
Sonoran Desert plant with a dramatic flower stalk
words.context: Multiple agave species native to the Sonoran Desert; iconic landscape plant in Tucson.

clue_styles: Spanish word in '___ Caliente' (eastside Tucson park)
What's scarce in the Sonoran Desert
words.context: Agua Caliente Park is a popular eastside Tucson park with natural hot springs.

clue_styles: Friendly Spanish greeting in a bilingual city
words.context: Tucson's Hispanic majority makes Spanish loanwords feel native, not exotic.

clue_styles: Like Tucson's climate, in one word
Dry, as the air for ten months
words.context: Tucson is a textbook arid climate (~12 inches of rain per year).

clue_styles: Grilled-meat style at any Tucson taqueria
words.context: Carne asada is the default taco protein in Sonoran-style Tucson Mexican food.

clue_styles: '___ Down' — the U of A rallying cry
Word that rallies the McKale Center
words.context: 'Bear Down' is the University of Arizona's official cheer, shouted at every Wildcats game. McKale Center is the basketball arena.

clue_styles: Spanish for good — heard often in Tucson
words.context: Common Spanish loanword in Tucson's bilingual register.

clue_styles: Beast of burden in Old Western Tucson
words.context: Old Tucson Studios (the Western film set west of the city) and ranching heritage make this fit.
Plural of cactus. Universally Tucson.

clue_styles: Spanish word before 'asada' on a Sonoran menu
words.context: Carne asada anchors Sonoran Mexican food in Tucson.

clue_styles: Spanish word in '___ Grande' (city north on I-10)
words.context: Casa Grande is a city ~70 miles north on I-10; common Tucson Spanish loanword.

clue_styles: What McKale Center fans cheer for
words.context: The U of A men's basketball team is universally 'the Cats' to fans.

clue_styles: Pepper that fires up local cuisine
words.context: Mexican-cuisine pepper. Be careful: in a Tucson puzzle prefer the food clue, not the country.

clue_styles: Cook-off staple at a Tucson rodeo
Bowl of red, in the Southwest
words.context: American spelling; less specifically Tucson than CHILE but works as Western/Southwestern fill.
Corn (maize) anchors Sonoran food via tortillas, elotes, masa.
Mule deer are common in the Sky Island ranges; Tanque Verde literally means 'green pool' from a deer-drinking spring.
Mourning doves and white-winged doves are everywhere in Tucson; their cooing is the soundtrack of the city. Dove Mountain is also a Marana landmark.

clue_styles: Frank Herbert setting that feels familiar in July
words.context: Generic desert fill; Tucson itself isn't dune country, so use lightly.
Same caveat as DUNE — generic Southwest fill.

clue_styles: What a haboob kicks up across I-10
words.context: Haboobs (massive dust storms) are a real Tucson summer phenomenon, often preceding monsoon storms.

clue_styles: Mexican street corn with cotija and chile
Cob of corn, dressed up Sonoran-style
words.context: Common Mexican street food in Tucson; corn on the cob with mayo, cotija cheese, lime, and chile powder.

clue_styles: Sudden, as a wash filling in July
words.context: Flash floods in dry washes are a real Tucson hazard during monsoon. 'Turn around, don't drown' is a local PSA staple.

clue_styles: What a wash does in a hard monsoon
Why Tucson signs say 'do not enter when wet'
words.context: Same monsoon-and-arroyo context as FLASH.
Red-tailed and Harris's hawks are common Sonoran Desert raptors. Harris's hawks are notable for hunting in family groups.

clue_styles: What hits Tucson by 9 a.m. in July
Reason for the Tucson summer schedule shift
words.context: Universal Tucson summer context; many locals adopt a sunrise-or-sunset schedule from June through September.
Sabino Canyon, Tumamoc Hill, Pima Canyon, the Catalinas — Tucson is a hiking city.
Common everyday Spanish in Tucson's bilingual fabric.

clue_styles: Metal pulled from Arizona ground, sometimes
words.context: Iron Horse is a well-known Tucson neighborhood near downtown, so named for the railroad.
Tucson is majority Hispanic/Latino; the cultural fabric is genuinely bilingual.

clue_styles: Mt. ___ — the Sky Island above Tucson

words.context: Mt. Lemmon (9,159 ft) sits above Tucson — named for botanist Sara Plummer Lemmon, the first woman to climb it. Locals love the citrus pun.

clue_styles: Citrus on the rim of a Tucson margarita
words.context: Universal accompaniment to Mexican food and drink.

clue_styles: Spanish for pretty — and a famous Tucson first name
words.context: Linda Ronstadt is from Tucson; her family is a multigenerational Tucson institution. Her brother was Tucson's police chief.

clue_styles: Spanish for crazy, as the August heat
words.context: Common Spanish loanword in Tucson register.

clue_styles: Spanish word for what U of A's Lunar & Planetary Lab studies
words.context: U of A's Lunar & Planetary Laboratory has been part of NASA missions since Apollo. Strong dual cultural+science angle.

clue_styles: Word in U of A's '___ & Planetary Laboratory'
words.context: U of A's Lunar & Planetary Lab is a major NASA partner; Tucson is a serious space-science city.

clue_styles: Tortilla dough at any Tucson tortilleria
words.context: Corn dough; foundation of tortillas and tamales. Fits Tucson's Sonoran food register.

clue_styles: City near Phoenix with a Spanish 'table' name
words.context: Generic Southwest geography; also an AZ city.

clue_styles: What pulls ore from Arizona ground
words.context: Active and historic copper mines surround Tucson. Asarco's Mission and Silver Bell mines are prominent.

clue_styles: Sauce with chocolate, chile, and patience
words.context: Traditional Mexican sauce; multiple regional styles.

clue_styles: What U of A scientists helped Apollo astronauts walk on

words.context: U of A's Lunar & Planetary Lab helped train Apollo astronauts and map the moon. Strong Tucson space-science angle.

clue_styles: Edible cactus pad on a Sonoran menu

words.context: Nopales (cactus pads) are a Sonoran/Mexican vegetable, common in Tucson kitchens and on menus.

clue_styles: Watering hole in the desert imagination
Agua Caliente Park, in a way
words.context: Generic desert imagery; Agua Caliente Park's springs make a soft local angle.

clue_styles: Path of OSIRIS-REx (a U of A-led NASA mission)
What U of A's space scientists track
words.context: OSIRIS-REx, the asteroid sample-return mission, was led by U of A's Dante Lauretta. Tucson has serious space chops.

clue_styles: Spanish for stick — and the start of a yellow-flowered Sonoran tree
words.context: Palo verde is Arizona's state tree; iconic green-trunked Sonoran Desert species. 'Palo' = Spanish for stick.
Saguaro National Park has east and west units flanking Tucson. Reid Park is the city's central park, with the zoo.

clue_styles: Cotton variety grown in southern Arizona
words.context: Pima County contains Tucson; the Pima Air & Space Museum is one of the largest in the world; Pima cotton (extra-long staple) is named for the Akimel O'odham (formerly 'Pima') people.
Pinto beans are the default refried bean in Sonoran cuisine.
Common Spanish menu word in Tucson taquerias and restaurants.

clue_styles: What every Tucsonan starts checking radar for in July
words.context: Monsoon rain (June 15 - Sept 30) is a beloved seasonal event; Tucsonans track radar obsessively.

clue_styles: What climbers grip in Mt. Lemmon's granite
words.context: Tucson has world-class rock climbing in the Catalinas and at Mt. Lemmon.

clue_styles: Brush in a Western movie set west of Tucson

clue_styles: Bowl that arrives before the entree at a Tucson taqueria

Beach absent from the Sonoran Desert

clue_styles: Type of panel on many Tucson rooftops

words.context: Tucson is one of the best solar markets in the US; rooftop panels are common.

clue_styles: Tucson night-sky pleasure (the city limits light pollution by ordinance)
words.context: Tucson has a strict dark-sky ordinance to protect Kitt Peak Observatory and other astronomy sites. Real local pride.

clue_styles: Sonoran handheld in a 4th Avenue lunch line

clue_styles: Colorado River ___ heard after a monsoon storm

Spanish for green — as in the spring after a wet winter
words.context: VERDE shows up in dozens of Tucson place names: Tanque Verde, Palo Verde, Verde Vista, etc. One of the most useful local words.

clue_styles: How city seats are organized in Tucson
words.context: Tucson City Council has six wards. Light civic angle — use sparingly to avoid making the puzzle feel governmental.

words.context: 'Local' as identity marker; reinforces Tucson's distinct sense of place vs. Phoenix.

thematic_lexicon.description: Tucson-iconic terms that are too long to be 5x5 grid answers (>5 letters) but should be biased into clue *text* whenever a clue can naturally reference them. The LLM should reach for these to make a Tucson reader smile, especially when the answer itself is generic. Example: clue for the answer COW could be 'Beast at La Fiesta de los Vaqueros' instead of 'Dairy farm resident.'

terms.context: The iconic 40-foot cactus that grows only in the Sonoran Desert; namesake of Saguaro National Park (East and West units flanking Tucson).
Whip-like Sonoran Desert plant with flame-red flowers in spring.
Native desert tree; provides shade, pods, and flour. Mesquite-grilled food is a local specialty.
Jumping cholla cactus — the segments seem to leap onto passing hikers.
The shrub responsible for the smell of the desert after a monsoon rain.
Common Sonoran cactus; pads (nopales) and fruit (tunas) are both eaten.
Tucson's beloved summer rainy season, June 15 - September 30. Locals track radar daily.
Massive dust storm that rolls across the Sonoran Desert before monsoon storms.
Spanish word for a sudden squall; sometimes used for Sonoran Desert summer storms.
Wild peccary that travels in herds through Tucson neighborhoods, especially at dawn and dusk.
Ubiquitous Tucson urban wildlife; you'll hear them at night.
Long-tailed ground bird; New Mexico's state bird but very much present in Tucson.
Wild cat seen occasionally in Tucson foothills neighborhoods.
Western diamondback rattlesnake — the snake locals warn newcomers about.
Venomous, beaded-skin lizard native to the Sonoran Desert. Slow-moving and rare.
Santa Catalina Mountains — the dramatic range north of Tucson. Includes Mt. Lemmon.
Rincon Mountains — Tucson's eastern range.
Mountain range south of Tucson; home to Madera Canyon and Mt. Wrightson.
Tumamoc Hill — beloved 3-mile paved walking hill on Tucson's west side.
Sabino Canyon — Tucson's most popular hiking destination, on the Catalinas' south flank.
Rillito River — the seasonal east-west river forming Tucson's northern boundary; its bike path is a local favorite.
Spanish for a dry desert creek bed; a 'wash' in local English.
Spanish for neighborhood; Tucson has historic Barrios Viejo, Anita, Hollywood, Santa Rosa, and others.
The original 1775 Spanish fort site at downtown Tucson; reconstructed as a museum.
1797 Spanish mission south of Tucson — 'White Dove of the Desert.' One of the great Spanish colonial buildings in North America.
Tucson's nickname, used affectionately by locals.
University of Arizona athletics teams.
U of A's official rallying cry.
U of A wildcat mascot (with WILMA, his counterpart).
Of or pertaining to the Sonoran Desert; also the regional cuisine style (Sonoran hot dog, Sonoran enchilada).
Tucson-born frozen drink chain (since 1971); a frosty, fruity slushy is just called 'an eegee.'
Bacon-wrapped hot dog with beans, onions, tomatoes, mayo, and salsa on a soft bun. Tucson street-food specialty.
Tucson's main bar/music/retail strip just west of U of A; '4th Ave' to locals.
Hotel Congress downtown — historic music venue and where John Dillinger was caught in 1934.
Speedway Boulevard — Tucson's main east-west arterial; once called one of America's ugliest streets by Life magazine, locals are fond of it anyway.
Catalina Foothills — the affluent area between central Tucson and the mountains.
Town northwest of Tucson; one of the four municipalities TDB covers.
Town directly north of Tucson; one of the four municipalities TDB covers.
Town south of Tucson; in Pima County.
National Observatory southwest of Tucson on Tohono O'odham land; reason for Tucson's dark-sky ordinance.
U of A-run Earth-systems science facility in Oracle, north of Tucson.
Arizona-Sonora Desert Museum — Tucson's beloved zoo/garden/natural history museum on the west side.
The Tucson Rodeo, every February since 1925; locals get rodeo days off school.
Tucson's annual community Day of the Dead-inspired procession (early November); a defining local cultural event.
Tucson Gem & Mineral Show, every February; the largest in the world. Often paired with rodeo as 'February in Tucson.'
